content:
Most of what you’re calling growth mindset is not growth mindset.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Consulting firms packaged it into half-day workshops, performance review templates listed it as a measurable competency, and learning management platforms offered modules on it. None of it had much to do with what Carol Dweck’s research actually showed.
By the early 2010s, growth mindset had crossed from education into corporate learning and development, carrying Dweck’s name and shedding most of her constraints. Leadership programs built competency frameworks around it. Hiring rubrics listed it as a trait to screen for. Performance management systems used it to distinguish employees who grew from employees who plateaued. It became a general-purpose account of why some people advance and others don’t: a portable theory of human potential, applicable to any industry, any role, any organization. The version that spread wasn’t wrong about everything. It was wrong about what the research actually showed, and under what conditions the finding held.
Dweck named the problem herself. Her January 2016 Edutopia piece described educators who “think and do all sorts of things that they simply call growth mindset.” She called it “false growth mindset,” acknowledging that what she introduced, stripped of constraints, no longer resembled what the research supported.

Dweck’s original finding was narrow by design. She and her collaborators measured the effect of feedback language on student persistence in structured learning environments. Students praised for effort rather than innate ability were more willing to attempt harder problems and less likely to retreat from difficulty. The intervention was a change in how adults spoke to children during practice. The measured outcome was willingness to attempt harder tasks. Not career advancement. Not professional performance under ambiguous conditions. Not organizational culture. Willingness to attempt harder problems, in contexts where effort produced measurable improvement.
That last condition is what most applications discard. That dependency wasn’t incidental to the finding.
The Mueller and Dweck study put that precision to work. Fifth graders completed a set of puzzle problems, received praise for their intelligence, praise for their effort, or neutral feedback on their score, and then worked through a second set designed to be difficult enough that every student would fail. The question was what that failure would do. Students praised for their intelligence responded by choosing easier problems next, reporting less enjoyment, and performing worse on the final set. Students praised for their effort chose harder problems, maintained their enjoyment, and recovered their performance. The study wasn’t measuring mindset as a general disposition. It was measuring the behavioral consequences of one specific kind of feedback, delivered during a specific kind of practice, in a controlled environment where the relationship between effort and outcome was clear.
Dweck’s mechanism required that relationship to be direct and legible. Praise for effort develops persistence only when the effort has somewhere to go. A student working through math problems gets better when the practice is structured and the feedback is real. Remove that relationship and the mechanism stops working. The research treated mindset as one variable among many. It mattered only when the environment was built to convert effort into growth.
The corporate version removed that condition and kept the vocabulary. Employees are told the right mindset will close skill gaps and produce career advancement. The workshop teaches them to reframe setbacks as learning opportunities and to praise their own effort regardless of outcome. That last instruction is what Dweck was specifically correcting in the Edutopia piece: effort praise divorced from actual learning or progress produces neither. The mechanism was environmental. The application made it attitudinal. Those are different interventions with different results.

The labor market data runs the same argument. As detailed in “AI Didn’t Kill Expertise,” the wage premium for AI-skilled workers concentrated not in those who adopted a growth mindset toward AI but in those with prior domain expertise, structural access to training, and organizational positions where new skills translated into value. Mindset didn’t produce that outcome. Context did. The workers who benefited were in environments where effort and skill development translated directly into measurable value. That’s the condition Dweck’s research always required. The corporate growth mindset framework sold it as optional.
I’ve watched organizations decline continuing education budgets and approve AI upskilling programs in the same fiscal year. AI reads as a tool acquisition. Growth mindset reads as an admission.

The problem with false growth mindset isn’t that it’s harmless. It redirects attention from structural factors to individual psychology. When performance gaps are explained by mindset, the solution becomes fixing the individual’s attitude rather than fixing the environment, the access, or the instruction. That move is convenient for organizations that benefit from locating failure in individuals rather than in resource allocation or systemic design.
Organizations that explain performance gaps through mindset don’t need to examine their training investment, their management quality, or the structural conditions that determine who gets access to meaningful development. Mindset becomes the explanation that makes every other explanation unnecessary. That’s a useful position for the organization. It’s not a useful position for the employees. The workers told to work on their mindset are handed a framework built for a specific kind of feedback in a controlled study, not for the structural reality they’re actually in.
Dweck’s concept described one specific mechanism by which feedback shapes persistence in environments built for learning. The mechanism is real. The extrapolation is not.
